abstract: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are emerging as highly promising materials for low-temperature heat transformation applications, including thermally driven adsorption chillers and heat pumps. Due to their exceptional microporosity and chemically tunable surfaces, MOFs can significantly outperform traditional adsorbents like silica gels and zeolites in terms of water uptake capacity and specialized adsorption behavior. This flexibility allows for the precise design of hydrophobic or hydrophilic frameworks tailored to specific operational conditions. By utilizing solar energy or industrial waste heat, MOF-based systems offer a sustainable alternative to conventional electricity-dependent heating and cooling technologies. This review examines the current state of MOF development for heat transformation, comparing their performance and hydrothermal stability with classical materials, while also discussing the critical role of shaping and coating techniques in optimizing heat and mass transfer.
